node_metadata.name: Aldesleukin
node_metadata.description: Aldesleukin, a lymphokine, is produced by recombinant DNA technology using a genetically engineered E. coli strain containing an analog of the human interleukin-2 gene. Genetic engineering techniques were used to modify the human IL-2 gene, and the resulting expression clone encodes a modified human interleukin-2. This recombinant form differs from native interleukin-2 in the following ways: a) Aldesleukin is not glycosylated because it is derived from E. coli; b) the molecule has no N-terminal alanine; the codon for this amino acid was deleted during the genetic engineering procedure; c) the molecule has serine substituted for cysteine at amino acid position 125. Aldesleukin binds to the IL-2 receptor which leads to heterodimerization of the cytoplasmic domains of the IL-2R beta and gamma(c) chains, activation of the tyrosine kinase Jak3, and phosphorylation of tyrosine residues on the IL-2R beta chain. These events led to the creation of an activated receptor complex, to which various cytoplasmic signaling molecules are recruited and become substrates for regulatory enzymes (especially tyrosine kinases) that are associated with the receptor. These events stimulate growth and differentiation of T cells. For treatment of adults with metastatic renal cell carcinoma.

node_metadata.name: acute myeloid leukemia with NPM1 somatic mutations
node_metadata.description: Acute myeloid leukemia with NPM1 somatic mutations is a subtype of acute myeloid leukemia with recurrent genetic abnormalities characterized by clonal proliferation of myeloid blasts harboring mutations of the NPM1 gene in the bone marrow, blood and other tissues. It is associated with multilineage dysplasia, involving the myeloid, monocytic, erythroid, and megakaryocytic cell lineages. Patients usually present with leukocytosis, thrombocytosis and nonspecific symptoms related to ineffective hematopoesis (fatigue, bleeding and bruising, recurrent infections, bone pain), with frequent extramedullary involvement typically presenting as gingival hyperplasia and lymphadenopathy.

node_metadata.name: Doxorubicin
node_metadata.description: Doxorubicin is a cytotoxic anthracycline antibiotic isolated from cultures of Streptomyces peucetius var. caesius. Doxorubicin binds to nucleic acids, presumably by specific intercalation of the planar anthracycline nucleus with the DNA double helix. Doxorubicin has antimitotic and cytotoxic activity through a number of proposed mechanisms of action: Doxorubicin forms complexes with DNA by intercalation between base pairs, and it inhibits topoisomerase II activity by stabilizing the DNA-topoisomerase II complex, preventing the religation portion of the ligation-religation reaction that topoisomerase II catalyzes. Doxorubicin is used to produce regression in disseminated neoplastic conditions like acute lymphoblastic leukemia, acute myeloblastic leukemia, Wilms’ tumor, neuroblastoma, soft tissue and bone sarcomas, breast carcinoma, ovarian carcinoma, transitional cell bladder carcinoma, thyroid carcinoma, gastric carcinoma, Hodgkin’s disease, malignant lymphoma and bronchogenic carcinoma in which the small cell histologic type is the most responsive compared to other cell types. Doxorubicin is also indicated for use as a component of adjuvant therapy in women with evidence of axillary lymph node involvement following resection of primary breast cancer.

pathway_summary: This recombinant interleukin-2 therapy activates IL-2 receptor signaling, promoting T-cell growth and differentiation. In the pathway, it is linked by therapeutic indication to a genetically defined subtype of acute myeloid leukemia characterized by mutations in a nucleolar protein gene and abnormal proliferation of myeloid blasts. Thus, the topic drug is connected to another medication entity through their shared use in treating this mutation-associated leukemia subtype.
explicit_query: Which cytotoxic anthracycline antibiotic that intercalates DNA and inhibits topoisomerase II shares an indication with Aldesleukin for acute myeloid leukemia with NPM1 somatic mutations?
implicit_query: Which anthracycline chemotherapeutic agent that intercalates into DNA and inhibits topoisomerase II is also used, like Aldesleukin, in treating the mutation-associated subtype of acute myeloid leukemia involving a nucleolar protein gene?
bare_query: Which cytotoxic anthracycline antibiotic used in the treatment of a wide range of cancers is associated with Aldesleukin?

node_metadata.name: Cefalotin
node_metadata.description: Cefalotin is a cephalosporin antibiotic. The bactericidal activity of cefalotin results from the inhibition of cell wall synthesis via affinity for penicillin-binding proteins (PBPs). The PBPs are transpeptidases which are vital in peptidoglycan biosynthesis. Therefore, their inhibition prevents this vital cell wall compenent from being properly synthesized. Used to prevent infection during surgery and to treat many kinds of infections of the blood, bone or joints, respiratory tract, skin, and urinary tract.

node_metadata.name: streptococcal pneumonia
node_metadata.description: A febrile disease caused by streptococcus pneumoniae. A febrile disease caused by streptococcus pneumoniae.

node_metadata.name: Linezolid
node_metadata.description: Linezolid is a synthetic antibiotic, the first of the oxazolidinone class, used for the treatment of infections caused by multi-resistant bacteria including streptococcus and methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA). The drug works by inhibiting the initiation of bacterial protein synthesis. Linezolid is a synthetic antibacterial agent of the oxazolidinone class of antibiotics. It has in vitro activity against aerobic Gram positive bacteria, certain Gram negative bacteria and anaerobic microorganisms. It selectively inhibits bacterial protein synthesis through binding to sites on the bacterial ribosome and prevents the formation of a functional 70S-initiation complex. Specifically, linezolid binds to a site on the bacterial 23S ribosomal RNA of the 50S subunit and prevents the formation of a functional 70S initiation complex, which is an essential component of the bacterial translation process. The results of time-kill studies have shown linezolid to be bacteriostatic against enterococci and staphylococci. For streptococci, linezolid was found to be bactericidal for the majority of strains. Linezolid is also a reversible, nonselective inhibitor of monoamine oxidase. Therefore, linezolid has the potential for interaction with adrenergic and serotonergic agents. For the treatment of bacterial infections caused by susceptible strains of vancomycin resistant Enterococcus faecium, Staphylococcal aureus (methicillin resistant and susceptible strains), Streptococcus pneumoniae, Streptococcus pyogenes, Streptococcus agalactiae.

pathway_summary: This first-generation cephalosporin antibiotic kills bacteria by binding penicillin-binding proteins and blocking peptidoglycan cell wall synthesis. Because it is used to treat respiratory infections such as the febrile lung infection caused by pneumococci, it is linked to another therapeutic agent category through their shared indication for that condition.
explicit_query: Which synthetic oxazolidinone antibiotic is indicated for streptococcal pneumonia, the same indication linked from Cefalotin, and acts by inhibiting initiation of bacterial protein synthesis at the 50S ribosomal subunit?
implicit_query: Which synthetic oxazolidinone antibiotic that inhibits formation of the bacterial 70S initiation complex is also used to treat pneumococcal febrile lung infections, a condition for which cefalotin may also be prescribed?
bare_query: Which synthetic oxazolidinone antibiotic used for multidrug-resistant Gram-positive infections, including MRSA and vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus faecium, is associated with Cefalotin?

node_metadata.name: Acetaminophen
node_metadata.description: Acetaminophen (paracetamol), also commonly known as _Tylenol_, is the most commonly taken analgesic worldwide and is recommended as first-line therapy in pain conditions by the World Health Organization (WHO). It is also used for its antipyretic effects, helping to reduce fever. This drug was initially approved by the U.S. FDA in 1951 and is available in a variety of forms including syrup form, regular tablets, effervescent tablets, injection, suppository, and other forms. According to its FDA labeling, acetaminophen's exact mechanism of action has not been fully established - despite this, it is often categorized alongside NSAIDs (nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs) due to its ability to inhibit the cyclooxygenase (COX) pathways. It is thought to exert central actions which ultimately lead to the alleviation of pain symptoms. In general, acetaminophen is used for the treatment of mild to moderate pain and reduction of fever. It is available over the counter in various forms, the most common being oral forms.

node_metadata.name: allergic rhinitis
node_metadata.description: Inflammation of the nasal mucous membranes caused by an IgE-mediated response to external allergens. The inflammation may also involve the mucous membranes of the sinuses, eyes, middle ear, and pharynx. Symptoms include sneezing, nasal congestion, rhinorrhea, and itching. It may lead to fatigue, drowsiness, and malaise thus causing impairment of the quality of life. Allergic rhinitis with a positive skin prick test to any allergen.

node_metadata.name: Dexbrompheniramine
node_metadata.description: Dexbrompheniramine maleate is an antihistamine agent that is used for the treatment of allergic conditions, such as hay fever or urticaria. Dexbrompheniramine competitively binds to the histamine H<sub>1</sub>-receptor. It competes with histamine for the normal H<sub>1</sub>-receptor sites on effector cells of the gastrointestinal tract, blood vessels and respiratory tract. This blocks the action of endogenous histamine, which subsequently leads to temporary relief of the negative symptoms brought on by histamine. For treatment and relief of symptoms of allergies, hay fever, and colds

pathway_summary: This widely used analgesic and antipyretic is linked through a shared therapeutic context involving IgE-mediated inflammation of the nasal mucosa triggered by environmental allergens. In other words, because it and another medication can both be used in the management of hay fever–type symptoms such as sneezing, congestion, runny nose, and itching, the pathway connects it to another pharmaceutical agent.
explicit_query: Which antihistamine agent is indicated for allergic rhinitis, the same indication linked to Acetaminophen, and works by competitively binding the histamine H1 receptor to relieve symptoms of hay fever and other allergies?
implicit_query: Which antihistamine, given as the maleate salt and acting as a competitive H1-receptor blocker, is used to relieve allergy and hay fever symptoms such as sneezing, runny nose, nasal congestion, and itching, and is therapeutically linked to acetaminophen through this shared symptom context?
bare_query: Which antihistamine drug used for allergies, hay fever, urticaria, and cold symptom relief is associated with Acetaminophen?

node_metadata.name: Atenolol
node_metadata.description: Atenolol is a cardioselective beta-blocker used in a variety of cardiovascular conditions. Atenolol is a cardioselective beta-blocker, called such because it selectively binds to the β1-adrenergic receptor as an antagonist up to a reported 26 fold more than β2 receptors. Selective activity at the β1 receptor produces cardioselectivity due to the higher population of this receptor in cardiac tissue. Some binding to β2 and possibly β3 receptors can still occur at therapeutic dosages but the effects mediated by antagonizing these are significantly reduced from those of non-selective agents. β1 and β2 receptors are G<sub>s</sub> coupled therefore antagonism of their activation reduces activity of adenylyl cyclase and its downstream signalling via cyclic adenosime monophosphate and protein kinase A (PKA). **Indicated** for:

node_metadata.name: hypertension
node_metadata.description: High blood pressure caused by an underlying medical condition. High blood pressure caused by an underlying medical condition.

node_metadata.name: Moxonidine
node_metadata.description: Moxonidine is a new-generation centrally acting antihypertensive drug approved for the treatment of mild to moderate essential hypertension. It is suggested to be effective in cases where other agents such as thiazides, beta-blockers, ACE inhibitors, and calcium channel blockers are not appropriate or irresponsive. As well, moxonidine has been shown to present blood pressure-independent beneficial effects on insulin resistance syndrome. Stimulation of central alpha 2-adrenergic receptors is associated with sympathoadrenal suppression and subsequent reduction of blood pressure. As this class was further explored it was discovered that sympathoadrenal activity can also be suppressed by a second pathway with a newly discovered drug target specific to imidazolines. Specifically, moxonidine binds the imidazoline receptor subtype 1 (I1) and to a lesser extent αlpha-2-adrenoreceptors in the RSV causing a reduction of sympathetic activity, reducing systemic vascular resistance and thus arterial blood pressure. For the treatment of mild to moderate essential or primary hypertension. Effective as most first-line antihypertensives when used as monotherapy.

pathway_summary: This cardioselective β1-adrenergic receptor blocker is used to treat elevated blood pressure. Because treatment of that cardiovascular condition is a standard therapeutic role for medications, the pathway links this agent to the broader category of pharmaceutical drugs.
explicit_query: Which centrally acting antihypertensive drug is also indicated for hypertension, the same indication as Atenolol, and is approved for treating mild to moderate essential hypertension?
implicit_query: Which newer centrally acting antihypertensive, approved for mild to moderate essential hypertension and acting mainly through imidazoline I1 receptors with some alpha-2 adrenergic activity, could be considered as an alternative to Atenolol when standard first-line agents are unsuitable or ineffective?
bare_query: Which new-generation centrally acting antihypertensive drug for mild to moderate essential hypertension is associated with Atenolol?

node_metadata.name: Iloperidone
node_metadata.description: Iloperidone is an atypical antipsychotic for the treatment of schizophrenia symptoms. Hoechst Marion Roussel Inc. researched the drug until May 1996. In June 1997 they gave the research rights to Titan Pharmaceuticals, who gave the worldwide development, manufacturing, and marketing rights to Novartis in August 1998. On June 9, 2004, Titan Pharmaceuticals gave the Phase III development rights to Vanda Pharmaceuticals. FDA approved on May 9, 2009. Iloperidone is a dopamine D2 and 5-HT2A receptor antagonist and acts as a neuroleptic agent. Treatment of acute schizophrenia.

node_metadata.name: schizophrenia
node_metadata.description: A major psychotic disorder characterized by abnormalities in the perception or expression of reality. It affects the cognitive and psychomotor functions. Common clinical signs and symptoms include delusions, hallucinations, disorganized thinking, and retreat from reality. A severe emotional disorder of psychotic depth characteristically marked by a retreat from reality with delusion formation, hallucinations, emotional disharmony, and regressive behavior.

node_metadata.name: Blonanserin
node_metadata.description: Blonanserin is an atypical antipsychotic approved in Japan in January, 2008. It offers improved tolerability as it lacks side effects such as extrapyramidal symptoms, excessive sedation, or hypotension. As a second-generation (atypical) antipsychotic, it is significantly more efficacious in the treatment of the negative symptoms of schizophrenia compared to first-generation (typical) antipsychotics. Blonanserin binds to and inhibits dopamine receptors D2 and D3 as well as the serotonin receptor 5-HT2A with high affinity. Blonanserin has low affinity for other dopamine and serotonin receptors as well as muscarinic, adrenergic, and histamine receptors. This reduces dopaminergic and serotonergic neurotransmission which is thought to produce a reduction in positive and negative symptoms of schizophrenia respectively. Used for the treatment of schizophrenia.

pathway_summary: Iloperidone is an atypical antipsychotic used to treat a severe psychotic disorder marked by hallucinations, delusions, disorganized thinking, and impaired reality testing. Because it is indicated for this condition, it is connected through that therapeutic use to the broader category of medications used to manage the disorder.
explicit_query: Which atypical antipsychotic is indicated for schizophrenia, like Iloperidone, and is approved in Japan with improved tolerability due to low risk of extrapyramidal symptoms, excessive sedation, and hypotension?
implicit_query: Which atypical antipsychotic approved in Japan in 2008, notable for high affinity for D2, D3, and 5-HT2A receptors and relatively low risk of extrapyramidal symptoms, excessive sedation, or hypotension, is also used to treat the same severe psychotic disorder as iloperidone?
bare_query: Which atypical antipsychotic approved in Japan in 2008 is related to Iloperidone and is known for treating schizophrenia with relatively good tolerability?

node_metadata.name: Promazine
node_metadata.description: A phenothiazine with actions similar to chlorpromazine but with less antipsychotic activity. It is primarily used in short-term treatment of disturbed behavior and as an antiemetic. It is currently not approved for use in the United States. Promazine is an antagonist at types 1, 2, and 4 dopamine receptors, 5-HT receptor types 2A and 2C, muscarinic receptors 1 through 5, alpha(1)-receptors, and histamine H1-receptors. Promazine's antipsychotic effect is due to antagonism at dopamine and serotonin type 2 receptors, with greater activity at serotonin 5-HT2 receptors than at dopamine type-2 receptors. This may explain the lack of extrapyramidal effects. Promazine does not appear to block dopamine within the tubero-infundibular tract, explaining the lower incidence of hyperprolactinemia than with typical antipsychotic agents or risperidone. Antagonism at muscarinic receptors, H1-receptors, and alpha(1)-receptors also occurs with promazine. Used as an adjunct for short term treatment of moderate and severe psychomotor agitation. Also used to treat agitation or restlessness in the elderly.

node_metadata.name: common cold
node_metadata.description: An inflammatory process affecting the nasal mucosa, usually caused by viruses (e.g., rhinovirus, adenovirus, parainfluenza virus, and coronavirus). It is characterized by chills, headaches, mucopurulent nasal discharge, coughing, and facial pain. A catarrhal disorder of the upper respiratory tract, which may be viral or a mixed infection. It generally involves a runny nose, nasal congestion, and sneezing.

node_metadata.name: Guaifenesin
node_metadata.description: Guaifenesin possesses a storied history, having been originally formally approved by the US FDA in 1952 and continues to be one of very few - if not perhaps the only drug that is readily available and used as an expectorant. Since that time the agent has been a combination component of various prescription and non-prescription over-the-counter cough and cold products and is currently a widely available over-the-counter generic medication. Although it is principally believed that guaifenesin elicits an action to facilitate productive cough to manage chest congestion , it is not known whether the agent can reliably mitigate coughing. Although the exact mechanism of action of guaifenesin may not yet be formally or totally elucidated, it is believed that expectorants like guaifenesin function by increasing mucus secretion. Moreover, it is also further proposed that such expectorants may also act as an irritant to gastric vagal receptors, and recruit efferent parasympathetic reflexes that can elicit glandular exocytosis that is comprised of a less viscous mucus mixture. Subsequently, these actions may provoke coughing that can ultimately flush difficult to access, congealed mucopurulent material from obstructed small airways to facilitate a temporary improvement for the individual. Guaifenesin is an expectorant that is indicated for providing temporary symptomatic relief from congested chests and coughs which may be due to a cold, bronchitis, and/or other breathing illnesses.

pathway_summary: This phenothiazine agent, best known for sedative, antiemetic, and dopamine/serotonin receptor–blocking effects, is linked through a respiratory syndrome involving inflammation of the nasal passages with congestion, sneezing, and rhinorrhea. That upper-airway viral illness is itself a condition for which medications are indicated, connecting the compound to another therapeutic agent via shared use in treating cold symptoms.
explicit_query: Which drug, indicated for the common cold like Promazine, is an expectorant used for temporary relief of chest congestion and cough by helping increase mucus secretion?
implicit_query: Which over-the-counter expectorant, first approved by the FDA in 1952 and still widely used in cough and cold combinations to help relieve chest congestion and promote productive cough, is connected to promazine through their shared use in treating symptoms of the common cold?
bare_query: Which over-the-counter expectorant, first approved by the FDA in 1952 and still widely used for temporary relief of chest congestion and cough, is associated with Promazine?

node_metadata.name: MAP3K6
node_metadata.description: This gene encodes a serine/threonine protein kinase that forms a component of protein kinase-mediated signal transduction cascades. The encoded kinase participates in the regulation of vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) expression. Alternative splicing results in multiple transcript variants. [provided by RefSeq, Jul 2014].

node_metadata.name: breast neoplasm
node_metadata.description: A benign or malignant neoplasm of the breast parenchyma. It can originate from the ducts, lobules or the breast adipose tissue. Breast neoplasms are much more common in females than males.

node_metadata.name: MIR429
node_metadata.description: microRNAs (miRNAs) are short (20-24 nt) non-coding RNAs that are involved in post-transcriptional regulation of gene expression in multicellular organisms by affecting both the stability and translation of mRNAs. miRNAs are transcribed by RNA polymerase II as part of capped and polyadenylated primary transcripts (pri-miRNAs) that can be either protein-coding or non-coding. The primary transcript is cleaved by the Drosha ribonuclease III enzyme to produce an approximately 70-nt stem-loop precursor miRNA (pre-miRNA), which is further cleaved by the cytoplasmic Dicer ribonuclease to generate the mature miRNA and antisense miRNA star (miRNA*) products. The mature miRNA is incorporated into a RNA-induced silencing complex (RISC), which recognizes target mRNAs through imperfect base pairing with the miRNA and most commonly results in translational inhibition or destabilization of the target mRNA. The RefSeq represents the predicted microRNA stem-loop. [provided by RefSeq, Sep 2009].

pathway_summary: MAP3K6 encodes a serine/threonine kinase involved in intracellular signaling and regulation of VEGF expression, linking it to pathways that influence cell growth and vascular responses. Because this kinase is associated with tumors arising in breast tissue, it is consequently connected to other protein-coding factors implicated in breast tumor biology.
explicit_query: Which microRNA, a short 20–24 nt non-coding RNA involved in post-transcriptional regulation of gene expression, is associated with breast neoplasm that is also associated with MAP3K6?
implicit_query: Which microRNA, a short non-coding RNA that represses gene expression after transcription, is linked to MAP3K6 through its roles in VEGF-related signaling and breast tumor biology?
bare_query: Which microRNA involved in post-transcriptional regulation is associated with MAP3K6?

node_metadata.name: MRPL9
node_metadata.description: This is a nuclear gene encoding a protein component of the 39S subunit of the mitochondrial ribosome. Alternative splicing results in multiple transcript variants. A pseudogene of this gene is found on chromosome 8. [provided by RefSeq, Jul 2014].

node_metadata.name: hereditary breast carcinoma
node_metadata.description: Breast carcinoma that has developed in relatives of patients with history of breast carcinoma.

node_metadata.name: COL19A1
node_metadata.description: This gene encodes the alpha chain of type XIX collagen, a member of the FACIT collagen family (fibril-associated collagens with interrupted helices). Although the function of this collagen is not known, other members of this collagen family are found in association with fibril-forming collagens such as type I and II, and serve to maintain the integrity of the extracellular matrix. The transcript produced from this gene has an unusually large 3' UTR which has not been completely sequenced. [provided by RefSeq, Jul 2008].

pathway_summary: This mitochondrial ribosomal large-subunit component, encoded in the nucleus, is linked to inherited forms of breast cancer. That disease context, in turn, is associated with other molecular factors of the gene/protein class, suggesting this mitochondrial translation-related factor may connect to protein-coding contributors involved in familial breast tumor susceptibility.
explicit_query: Which gene/protein is associated with hereditary breast carcinoma along with MRPL9 and encodes the alpha chain of type XIX collagen, a FACIT family member involved in maintaining extracellular matrix integrity?
implicit_query: Which gene encoding the alpha chain of a type XIX FACIT collagen involved in maintaining extracellular matrix integrity is linked, through inherited breast cancer susceptibility pathways, to the mitochondrial ribosomal protein MRPL9?
bare_query: Which gene encoding the alpha chain of a type XIX collagen involved in maintaining extracellular matrix integrity is associated with MRPL9?

node_metadata.name: SORT1
node_metadata.description: This gene encodes a member of the VPS10-related sortilin family of proteins. The encoded preproprotein is proteolytically processed by furin to generate the mature receptor. This receptor plays a role in the trafficking of different proteins to either the cell surface, or subcellular compartments such as lysosomes and endosomes. Expression levels of this gene may influence the risk of myocardial infarction in human patients. Alternative splicing results in multiple transcript variants. [provided by RefSeq, Oct 2015].

node_metadata.name: coronary artery disease
node_metadata.description: Narrowing of the coronary arteries due to fatty deposits inside the arterial walls. The diagnostic criteria may include documented history of any of the following: documented coronary artery stenosis greater than or equal to 50% (by cardiac catheterization or other modality of direct imaging of the coronary arteries); previous coronary artery bypass surgery (CABG); previous percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI); previous myocardial infarction. (ACC) An imbalance between myocardial functional requirements and the capacity of the coronary vessels to supply sufficient blood flow. It is a form of myocardial ischemia caused by a decreased capacity of the coronary vessels.

node_metadata.name: ATP1A1
node_metadata.description: The protein encoded by this gene belongs to the family of P-type cation transport ATPases, and to the subfamily of Na+/K+ -ATPases. Na+/K+ -ATPase is an integral membrane protein responsible for establishing and maintaining the electrochemical gradients of Na and K ions across the plasma membrane. These gradients are essential for osmoregulation, for sodium-coupled transport of a variety of organic and inorganic molecules, and for electrical excitability of nerve and muscle. This enzyme is composed of two subunits, a large catalytic subunit (alpha) and a smaller glycoprotein subunit (beta). The catalytic subunit of Na+/K+ -ATPase is encoded by multiple genes. This gene encodes an alpha 1 subunit. Multiple transcript variants encoding different isoforms have been found for this gene. [provided by RefSeq, May 2009].

pathway_summary: A VPS10-domain sorting receptor involved in directing proteins to the cell surface and to endosomal/lysosomal compartments has been linked to risk of coronary atherosclerotic narrowing and myocardial infarction. Because this ischemic heart condition is itself associated with additional molecular factors, the pathway suggests that this trafficking receptor is indirectly connected to another protein through their shared relationship with coronary artery disease.
explicit_query: Which gene/protein associated with coronary artery disease is linked to SORT1 and encodes the alpha-1 catalytic subunit of the Na+/K+-ATPase, a P-type cation transport ATPase that maintains Na+ and K+ electrochemical gradients across the plasma membrane?
implicit_query: Which plasma membrane alpha subunit of the Na+/K+-transporting ATPase, essential for maintaining sodium and potassium electrochemical gradients and supporting nerve and muscle excitability, is indirectly linked to SORT1 through their shared association with coronary artery disease?
bare_query: Which membrane protein involved in maintaining sodium and potassium ion gradients across the plasma membrane is associated with SORT1?

juvenile arthritis due to defect in LACC1

node_metadata.name: FAXDC2
node_metadata.description: Predicted to enable C-4 methylsterol oxidase activity. Involved in positive regulation of megakaryocyte differentiation and positive regulation of protein phosphorylation. Located in cytoplasm. [provided by Alliance of Genome Resources, Apr 2022]

node_metadata.name: juvenile arthritis due to defect in LACC1
node_metadata.description: Arthritis in children, with onset before 16 years of age. The terms juvenile rheumatoid arthritis and juvenile idiopathic arthritis refer to classification systems for chronic arthritis in children. Only one subtype of juvenile arthritis clinically resembles adult rheumatoid arthritis and is considered its childhood equivalent.

node_metadata.name: FAM20A
node_metadata.description: This locus encodes a protein that is likely secreted and may function in hematopoiesis. A mutation at this locus has been associated with amelogenesis imperfecta and gingival hyperplasia syndrome. Alternatively spliced transcript variants have been identified. [provided by RefSeq, Aug 2011].

pathway_summary: FAXDC2 is linked to a childhood-onset chronic inflammatory joint disorder that begins before age 16 and includes forms resembling the pediatric counterpart of adult rheumatoid arthritis. That disease, in turn, is associated with a gene/protein factor, suggesting an indirect connection between FAXDC2 and a protein implicated in juvenile inflammatory arthritis biology.
explicit_query: Which gene/protein is associated with juvenile arthritis due to defect in LACC1 and encodes a likely secreted protein that may function in hematopoiesis, with mutations linked to amelogenesis imperfecta and gingival hyperplasia syndrome, in relation to FAXDC2?
implicit_query: Which secreted protein-coding gene, potentially involved in hematopoiesis and also linked through mutation to amelogenesis imperfecta with gingival hyperplasia, is indirectly connected to FAXDC2 in the context of childhood-onset chronic inflammatory arthritis beginning before age 16?
bare_query: Which gene/protein is associated with FAXDC2 and is described as a likely secreted factor that may function in hematopoiesis, with mutations linked to amelogenesis imperfecta and gingival hyperplasia syndrome?

node_metadata.name: MT1A
node_metadata.description: This gene is a member of the metallothionein family of genes. Proteins encoded by this gene family are low in molecular weight, are cysteine-rich, lack aromatic residues, and bind divalent heavy metal ions. The conserved cysteine residues co-ordinate metal ions using mercaptide linkages. These proteins act as anti-oxidants, protect against hydroxyl free radicals, are important in homeostatic control of metal in the cell, and play a role in detoxification of heavy metals. Disruption of two metallothionein genes in mouse resulted in defects in protection against heavy metals, oxidative stress, immune reactions, carcinogens, and displayed obesity. [provided by RefSeq, Sep 2017].

node_metadata.name: fibrolamellar hepatocellular carcinoma
node_metadata.description: A distinctive type of liver cell carcinoma that arises in non-cirrhotic livers and is seen predominantly in young patients. The tumor cells are polygonal and deeply eosinophilic, and are embedded in a fibrous stroma. The prognosis is similar to classical hepatocellular carcinoma that arises in non-cirrhotic livers, and better than hepatocellular carcinoma that arises in cirrhotic livers.

node_metadata.name: CRHBP
node_metadata.description: Corticotropin-releasing hormone is a potent stimulator of synthesis and secretion of preopiomelanocortin-derived peptides. Although CRH concentrations in the human peripheral circulation are normally low, they increase throughout pregnancy and fall rapidly after parturition. Maternal plasma CRH probably originates from the placenta. Human plasma contains a CRH-binding protein which inactivates CRH and which may prevent inappropriate pituitary-adrenal stimulation in pregnancy. [provided by RefSeq, Jul 2008].

pathway_summary: MT1A encodes a small, cysteine-rich metal-binding protein involved in antioxidant defense, detoxification of heavy metals, and maintenance of cellular metal balance. Its association with a rare liver cancer that typically occurs in young people without cirrhosis suggests that altered metal handling and oxidative-stress responses may be linked to a molecular factor in this tumor context.
explicit_query: Which gene/protein is associated with fibrolamellar hepatocellular carcinoma in relation to MT1A and encodes the plasma corticotropin-releasing hormone-binding protein that inactivates CRH and may help prevent inappropriate pituitary-adrenal stimulation during pregnancy?
implicit_query: Which gene encoding the plasma binding protein that inactivates corticotropin-releasing hormone and helps prevent inappropriate pituitary-adrenal stimulation during pregnancy has been implicated in the tumor context associated with MT1A dysregulation?
bare_query: Which gene/protein is associated with MT1A and is known for encoding a plasma binding protein that helps regulate corticotropin-releasing hormone levels, particularly during pregnancy?

node_metadata.name: FAXDC2
node_metadata.description: Predicted to enable C-4 methylsterol oxidase activity. Involved in positive regulation of megakaryocyte differentiation and positive regulation of protein phosphorylation. Located in cytoplasm. [provided by Alliance of Genome Resources, Apr 2022]

node_metadata.name: juvenile idiopathic arthritis
node_metadata.description: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA) is the term used to describe a group of inflammatory articular disorders of unknown cause that begin before the age of 16 and last over 6 weeks. The term juvenile idiopathic arthritis was chosen to signify the absence of any known mechanism underlying the disorder and to highlight the necessity of excluding other types of arthritis occurring in well defined diseases (in particular arthritis occurring in association with infectious, inflammatory and haematooncologic diseases). A group of conditions used to describe polyarthritis occurring in children.

node_metadata.name: SLC22A16
node_metadata.description: This gene encodes a member of the organic zwitterion transporter protein family which transports carnitine. The encoded protein has also been shown to transport anticancer drugs like bleomycin (PMID: 20037140) successful treatment has been correlated with the level of activity of this transporter in tumor cells. [provided by RefSeq, Dec 2011].

pathway_summary: This cytoplasmic factor, predicted to have C-4 methylsterol oxidase activity and to promote megakaryocyte differentiation as well as protein phosphorylation, is linked to juvenile idiopathic arthritis. That pediatric inflammatory joint disorder, characterized by chronic arthritis beginning before age 16, is in turn associated with another molecular factor of the gene/protein class.
explicit_query: Which gene/protein, associated with juvenile idiopathic arthritis and linked to FAXDC2, encodes an organic zwitterion transporter that transports carnitine and has also been shown to transport anticancer drugs such as bleomycin?
implicit_query: Which carnitine-transporting organic zwitterion transporter, also known to mediate uptake of anticancer agents such as bleomycin, is associated through juvenile idiopathic arthritis with the cytoplasmic factor FAXDC2?
bare_query: Which gene/protein is associated with FAXDC2 and is known as an organic zwitterion transporter involved in carnitine transport, with reported links to the uptake of anticancer drugs such as bleomycin?

node_metadata.name: MT1A
node_metadata.description: This gene is a member of the metallothionein family of genes. Proteins encoded by this gene family are low in molecular weight, are cysteine-rich, lack aromatic residues, and bind divalent heavy metal ions. The conserved cysteine residues co-ordinate metal ions using mercaptide linkages. These proteins act as anti-oxidants, protect against hydroxyl free radicals, are important in homeostatic control of metal in the cell, and play a role in detoxification of heavy metals. Disruption of two metallothionein genes in mouse resulted in defects in protection against heavy metals, oxidative stress, immune reactions, carcinogens, and displayed obesity. [provided by RefSeq, Sep 2017].

node_metadata.name: pediatric hepatocellular carcinoma
node_metadata.description: Pediatric hepatocellular carcinoma (pediatric HCC) is a rare, aggressive, malignant hepatic tumor that develops mainly in children over 10 years of age. HCC mostly develops in pediatric patients with no underlying liver disease, unlike in adults, when it is usually associated with hepatic cirrhosis due to alcohol intake. Pediatric HCC is mostly found in children and adolescents and rarely in children under 5 years of age. The main presenting manifestations are abdominal mass with pain, swelling and discomfort, weight loss, and anorexia. Splenomegaly, nausea, vomiting and jaundice are less commonly observed. Metastases to the mediastinal lymph nodes, lungs, brain and bone marrow are common in advanced disease (25% at presentation). HCC may be associated with congenital diseases, such as tyrosinemia, biliary atresia, tyrosinemia type 1 (in 50% of cases), alpha-1-antitrypsin deficiency, progressive familial intrahepatic cholestasis, Alagille or Gardner syndrome, Wilson or glycogen storage diseases I-IV, Fanconi anemia, familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP), focal nodular hyperplasia and hemochromatosis. A specific HCC variant, fibrolamellar carcinoma (FLC), occurs in older children and young adults, and has an equally dismal prognosis despite a slower growth and less tendency to metastasize.

node_metadata.name: NFKBIA
node_metadata.description: This gene encodes a member of the NF-kappa-B inhibitor family, which contain multiple ankrin repeat domains. The encoded protein interacts with REL dimers to inhibit NF-kappa-B/REL complexes which are involved in inflammatory responses. The encoded protein moves between the cytoplasm and the nucleus via a nuclear localization signal and CRM1-mediated nuclear export. Mutations in this gene have been found in ectodermal dysplasia anhidrotic with T-cell immunodeficiency autosomal dominant disease. [provided by RefSeq, Aug 2011].

pathway_summary: A cysteine-rich, metal-binding antioxidant protein involved in heavy-metal detoxification and cellular redox balance is linked to the rare, aggressive liver cancer seen in children and adolescents. This connection suggests that genes or proteins associated with pediatric liver tumor biology may relate to disrupted metal homeostasis, oxidative stress protection, or detoxification pathways in this disease.
explicit_query: Which NF-kappa-B inhibitor family gene, characterized by multiple ankyrin repeat domains and regulation of NF-kappa-B/REL complexes, is associated with pediatric hepatocellular carcinoma that is also associated with MT1A?
implicit_query: Which NF-kappa-B inhibitor family gene, encoding an ankyrin repeat-containing protein that shuttles between the cytoplasm and nucleus to restrain REL-containing inflammatory signaling complexes, is associated with MT1A in the context of pediatric hepatoblastoma biology?
bare_query: Which gene/protein is associated with MT1A and encodes an ankyrin repeat-containing inhibitor involved in inflammatory responses and linked to autosomal dominant ectodermal dysplasia with T-cell immunodeficiency?

node_metadata.name: DNAJC3
node_metadata.description: This gene encodes a protein with multiple tetratricopeptide repeat (TPR) motifs as well as the highly conserved J domain found in DNAJ chaperone family members. It is a member of the tetratricopeptide repeat family of proteins and acts as an inhibitor of the interferon-induced, dsRNA-activated protein kinase (PKR). [provided by RefSeq, Jul 2010].

node_metadata.name: prostate cancer
node_metadata.description: A primary or metastatic malignant tumor involving the prostate gland. The vast majority are carcinomas.

node_metadata.name: SLC39A1
node_metadata.description: This gene encodes a member of the zinc-iron permease family. The encoded protein is localized to the cell membrane and acts as a zinc uptake transporter. This gene has been linked to prostate cancer, breast cancer, and Alzheimer's disease. Alternative splicing results in multiple transcript variants. [provided by RefSeq, Dec 2012].

pathway_summary: DNAJC3 encodes a TPR-containing co-chaperone with a conserved J domain that can inhibit the interferon-inducible, double-stranded RNA-activated protein kinase pathway. Its reported association with malignant tumors of the prostate suggests it may be linked to other molecular factors in this cancer context, connecting it to another gene or protein involved in prostate tumor biology.
explicit_query: Which gene/protein, a cell membrane zinc uptake transporter in the zinc-iron permease family, is associated with prostate cancer and thereby linked to DNAJC3?
implicit_query: Which membrane-localized zinc uptake transporter from the zinc-iron permease family, also implicated in prostate cancer, is associated with DNAJC3 in the context of prostate tumor biology?
bare_query: Which gene/protein is associated with DNAJC3 and encodes a cell membrane zinc uptake transporter in the zinc-iron permease family that has been linked to prostate cancer, breast cancer, and Alzheimer's disease?

node_metadata.name: FAXDC2
node_metadata.description: Predicted to enable C-4 methylsterol oxidase activity. Involved in positive regulation of megakaryocyte differentiation and positive regulation of protein phosphorylation. Located in cytoplasm. [provided by Alliance of Genome Resources, Apr 2022]

node_metadata.name: spondyloarthropathy
node_metadata.description: A group of inflammatory rheumatic diseases associated with arthritis and enthesitis, and often involving the axial skeleton. The most common form of spondyloarthritis is ankylosing spondylitis. Other forms include axial spondyloarthritis, peripheral spondyloarthritis, reactive arthritis, psoriatic arthritis/spondylitis and enteropathic arthritis/spondylitis. Inflammation of the spine. This includes both arthritic and non-arthritic conditions.

node_metadata.name: CREM
node_metadata.description: This gene encodes a bZIP transcription factor that binds to the cAMP responsive element found in many viral and cellular promoters. It is an important component of cAMP-mediated signal transduction during the spermatogenetic cycle, as well as other complex processes. Alternative promoter and translation initiation site usage allows this gene to exert spatial and temporal specificity to cAMP responsiveness. Multiple alternatively spliced transcript variants encoding several different isoforms have been found for this gene, with some of them functioning as activators and some as repressors of transcription. [provided by RefSeq, Jul 2008].

pathway_summary: This cytoplasmic factor is predicted to participate in sterol-related oxidation and to promote both megakaryocyte maturation and protein phosphorylation. It is linked to a family of inflammatory rheumatic disorders characterized by arthritis, enthesitis, and frequent involvement of the spine, indicating a connection between this molecule and a broader protein-level disease network in spinal inflammatory arthritis.
explicit_query: Which gene/protein is associated with spondyloarthropathy and is also linked to FAXDC2, encoding a bZIP transcription factor that binds the cAMP responsive element in many viral and cellular promoters?
implicit_query: Which bZIP transcription factor that recognizes cAMP response elements in viral and cellular promoters is connected in the broader spinal inflammatory arthritis–related protein network to FAXDC2, a cytoplasmic factor implicated in sterol oxidation, megakaryocyte maturation, and protein phosphorylation?
bare_query: Which bZIP transcription factor involved in cAMP-responsive regulation during spermatogenesis is associated with FAXDC2?

node_metadata.name: MT1A
node_metadata.description: This gene is a member of the metallothionein family of genes. Proteins encoded by this gene family are low in molecular weight, are cysteine-rich, lack aromatic residues, and bind divalent heavy metal ions. The conserved cysteine residues co-ordinate metal ions using mercaptide linkages. These proteins act as anti-oxidants, protect against hydroxyl free radicals, are important in homeostatic control of metal in the cell, and play a role in detoxification of heavy metals. Disruption of two metallothionein genes in mouse resulted in defects in protection against heavy metals, oxidative stress, immune reactions, carcinogens, and displayed obesity. [provided by RefSeq, Sep 2017].

node_metadata.name: liver cancer
node_metadata.description: An epithelial or non-epithelial malignant neoplasm that arises from the liver. Representative examples include hepatocellular carcinoma, intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, lymphoma, and sarcoma.

node_metadata.name: TOP1
node_metadata.description: This gene encodes a DNA topoisomerase, an enzyme that controls and alters the topologic states of DNA during transcription. This enzyme catalyzes the transient breaking and rejoining of a single strand of DNA which allows the strands to pass through one another, thus altering the topology of DNA. This gene is localized to chromosome 20 and has pseudogenes which reside on chromosomes 1 and 22. [provided by RefSeq, Jul 2008].

pathway_summary: This metal-binding, cysteine-rich antioxidant protein is linked to malignant tumors arising in the liver. Through that disease association, it connects to another molecular factor of the gene/protein class involved in liver cancer biology.
explicit_query: Which gene/protein is associated with liver cancer in relation to MT1A and encodes a DNA topoisomerase that transiently breaks and rejoins single-stranded DNA during transcription to alter DNA topology?
implicit_query: Which DNA topoisomerase gene that transiently breaks and rejoins a single DNA strand during transcription is linked to liver cancer biology in connection with MT1A?
bare_query: Which gene/protein is associated with MT1A and encodes a DNA topoisomerase involved in controlling DNA topology during transcription?

node_metadata.name: PI4KA
node_metadata.description: This gene encodes a phosphatidylinositol (PI) 4-kinase which catalyzes the first committed step in the biosynthesis of phosphatidylinositol 4,5-bisphosphate. The mammalian PI 4-kinases have been classified into two types, II and III, based on their molecular mass, and modulation by detergent and adenosine. The protein encoded by this gene is a type III enzyme that is not inhibited by adenosine. Alternative splicing results in multiple transcript variants encoding distinct isoforms. [provided by RefSeq, Apr 2018].

node_metadata.name: schizophrenia
node_metadata.description: A major psychotic disorder characterized by abnormalities in the perception or expression of reality. It affects the cognitive and psychomotor functions. Common clinical signs and symptoms include delusions, hallucinations, disorganized thinking, and retreat from reality. A severe emotional disorder of psychotic depth characteristically marked by a retreat from reality with delusion formation, hallucinations, emotional disharmony, and regressive behavior.

node_metadata.name: OXT
node_metadata.description: This gene encodes a precursor protein that is processed to produce oxytocin and neurophysin I. Oxytocin is a posterior pituitary hormone which is synthesized as an inactive precursor in the hypothalamus along with its carrier protein neurophysin I. Together with neurophysin, it is packaged into neurosecretory vesicles and transported axonally to the nerve endings in the neurohypophysis, where it is either stored or secreted into the bloodstream. The precursor seems to be activated while it is being transported along the axon to the posterior pituitary. This hormone contracts smooth muscle during parturition and lactation. It is also involved in cognition, tolerance, adaptation and complex sexual and maternal behaviour, as well as in the regulation of water excretion and cardiovascular functions. [provided by RefSeq, Dec 2013].

pathway_summary: PI4KA encodes a type III phosphatidylinositol 4-kinase that initiates production of key phosphoinositide signaling lipids. Because this enzyme is associated with a severe psychotic disorder marked by disrupted perception, cognition, and behavior, it is plausibly connected to other molecular factors implicated in that same neuropsychiatric condition. Thus, the pathway links PI4KA to another protein-coding factor through their shared association with schizophrenia.
explicit_query: Which gene/protein, associated with schizophrenia in relation to PI4KA, encodes the precursor that is processed into oxytocin and neurophysin I, a posterior pituitary hormone involved in parturition, lactation, cognition, and maternal behavior?
implicit_query: Which hypothalamic precursor protein, processed into a posterior pituitary hormone involved in parturition, lactation, social behavior, and cognition, is plausibly linked to PI4KA through their shared association with schizophrenia?
bare_query: Which gene or protein is linked to PI4KA and encodes a precursor hormone involved in parturition, lactation, social behavior, and water balance?

node_metadata.name: VAV3
node_metadata.description: This gene is a member of the VAV gene family. The VAV proteins are guanine nucleotide exchange factors (GEFs) for Rho family GTPases that activate pathways leading to actin cytoskeletal rearrangements and transcriptional alterations. This gene product acts as a GEF preferentially for RhoG, RhoA, and to a lesser extent, RAC1, and it associates maximally with the nucleotide-free states of these GTPases. Alternatively spliced transcript variants encoding different isoforms have been described for this gene. [provided by RefSeq, Jul 2008].

node_metadata.name: prostate cancer
node_metadata.description: A primary or metastatic malignant tumor involving the prostate gland. The vast majority are carcinomas.

node_metadata.name: NDST4
node_metadata.description: Predicted to enable [heparan sulfate]-glucosamine N-sulfotransferase activity and deacetylase activity. Predicted to be involved in heparan sulfate proteoglycan biosynthetic process, polysaccharide chain biosynthetic process. Predicted to be located in Golgi membrane. Predicted to be integral component of membrane. Predicted to be active in Golgi apparatus. [provided by Alliance of Genome Resources, Apr 2022]

pathway_summary: This signaling factor is a guanine nucleotide exchange protein that activates Rho-family GTPases, influencing cytoskeletal remodeling and transcriptional programs. Because it is associated with malignant tumors of the prostate gland, it is implicated in disease-linked molecular networks and thus connects to other protein-coding molecules involved in prostate cancer biology.
explicit_query: Which gene/protein is associated with prostate cancer along with VAV3 and is predicted to have heparan sulfate-glucosamine N-sulfotransferase and deacetylase activity in the Golgi membrane, participating in heparan sulfate proteoglycan biosynthesis?
implicit_query: Which Golgi-resident integral membrane enzyme involved in heparan sulfate proteoglycan biosynthesis and possessing glucosamine N-sulfotransferase/deacetylase activity is connected to VAV3 in molecular networks linked to prostate cancer?
bare_query: Which gene is associated with VAV3 and is predicted to function as a Golgi membrane protein involved in heparan sulfate and polysaccharide biosynthesis?

node_metadata.name: GSK3B
node_metadata.description: The protein encoded by this gene is a serine-threonine kinase belonging to the glycogen synthase kinase subfamily. It is a negative regulator of glucose homeostasis and is involved in energy metabolism, inflammation, ER-stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, and apoptotic pathways. Defects in this gene have been associated with Parkinson disease and Alzheimer disease. [provided by RefSeq, Aug 2017].

node_metadata.name: B-cell neoplasm
node_metadata.description: A group of heterogeneous lymphoid tumors generally expressing one or more B-cell antigens or representing malignant transformations of B-lymphocytes.

node_metadata.name: BRAF
node_metadata.description: This gene encodes a protein belonging to the RAF family of serine/threonine protein kinases. This protein plays a role in regulating the MAP kinase/ERK signaling pathway, which affects cell division, differentiation, and secretion. Mutations in this gene, most commonly the V600E mutation, are the most frequently identified cancer-causing mutations in melanoma, and have been identified in various other cancers as well, including non-Hodgkin lymphoma, colorectal cancer, thyroid carcinoma, non-small cell lung carcinoma, hairy cell leukemia and adenocarcinoma of lung. Mutations in this gene are also associated with cardiofaciocutaneous, Noonan, and Costello syndromes, which exhibit overlapping phenotypes. A pseudogene of this gene has been identified on the X chromosome. [provided by RefSeq, Aug 2017].

pathway_summary: This serine/threonine kinase, which regulates glucose metabolism, inflammatory signaling, cellular stress responses, and apoptosis, is linked to malignancies arising from antibody-producing lymphocytes. That connection indicates it is associated with another molecular factor of the gene/protein class through its involvement in these B-lymphocyte–derived cancers.
explicit_query: Which RAF family serine/threonine protein kinase, commonly mutated as V600E and associated with B-cell neoplasm, is linked to GSK3B through an "associated with" relationship?
implicit_query: Which RAF-family serine/threonine kinase, best known for activating MAPK/ERK signaling and for the common V600E oncogenic mutation, is associated with GSK3B through their shared involvement in B-cell–derived malignancies such as non-Hodgkin lymphoma?
bare_query: Which RAF family serine/threonine protein kinase, frequently altered in melanoma and other cancers, is associated with GSK3B?

It is a negative regulator of glucose homeostasis and is involved in energy metabolism, inflammation, ER-stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, and apoptotic pathways. Defects in this gene have been associated with Parkinson disease and Alzheimer disease. [provided by RefSeq, Aug 2017].

node_metadata.name: major depressive disorder
node_metadata.description: An episode of depression lasting two or more weeks without an intervening episode of mania. An episode of depression lasting two or more weeks without an intervening episode of mania.

node_metadata.name: SLC6A13
node_metadata.description: Enables amino acid transmembrane transporter activity and monocarboxylic acid transmembrane transporter activity. Involved in amino acid import across plasma membrane and monocarboxylic acid transport. Located in extracellular exosome. [provided by Alliance of Genome Resources, Apr 2022]

pathway_summary: This serine/threonine kinase, which regulates glucose balance, energy metabolism, inflammation, cellular stress responses, mitochondrial function, and apoptosis, is linked to a prolonged depressive illness characterized by at least two weeks of low mood without mania. That disorder, in turn, is associated with another molecular factor of the gene/protein class, suggesting that this kinase may connect to additional protein-level mechanisms involved in depressive pathobiology.
explicit_query: Which gene/protein, associated with major depressive disorder and linked to GSK3B through this association, enables amino acid transmembrane transporter activity and monocarboxylic acid transmembrane transporter activity?
implicit_query: Which gene/protein involved in amino acid and monocarboxylic acid transport across the plasma membrane has been implicated in the depressive disorder associated with GSK3B?
bare_query: Which gene/protein is linked to GSK3B and is associated with amino acid and monocarboxylic acid transmembrane transport across the plasma membrane?

node_metadata.name: GSK3B
